claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 10 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes, I'd like to know if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child, Alex, who is 10 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 10 years old.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask: Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. And do you live with Alex, or are you paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. I do help out with some money for things like clothes and essentials, but it's not a huge amount - I give about £20 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility, I need to know: Does Alex live with someone else who might be claiming Child Benefit for them, such as their other parent or a relative?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And do you know if the other parent is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not entirely sure if they are or not to be honest.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's okay. Let me ask another question to assess your situation: How long has Alex been living with their other parent?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been living with their other parent for quite a while now - it's been the main arrangement for some time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. One more important question: You mentioned you contribute about £20 a week - is that the total amount you're contributing towards Alex's upkeep, including things like clothes, food, pocket money, and any other expenses?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - about £20 a week is what I'm able to contribute towards their needs at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

While Alex is 10 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16), to qualify for Child Benefit you must be "responsible" for the child. This means you need to either:
1. Live with them, **or**
2. Pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them

Since Alex lives with their other parent and not with you, you would need to meet the second condition. The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child. You're contributing £20 per week, which is less than this amount, so unfortunately you don't meet the responsibility test.

Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since Alex lives permanently with their other parent, that parent would be considered the responsible person and would be the appropriate claimant for Child Benefit.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes to live with you, or if you increase your contributions to at least match the Child Benefit rate - your eligibility could be different.

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), so meets the age requirement. However, to qualify for Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means either: (1) living with them, or (2) paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. Alex does not live with you - they live with their other parent. You are contributing £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep. The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child. Since your contribution of £20 per week is less than the Child Benefit amount, you do not meet the responsibility test. Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and since Alex lives with their other parent on a permanent basis, the other parent would be the person considered responsible for Alex and would be the appropriate claimant. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 10), who lives permanently with their other parent. While the claimant contributes £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is below the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an only child. Since the claimant doesn't live with Alex and isn't contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit, they don't meet the responsibility test. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the other parent who Alex lives with would be the appropriate claimant.

Alex does not live with you - they live with their other parent. You are contributing £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep. The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child. Since your contribution of £20 per week is less than the Child Benefit amount, you do not meet the responsibility test. Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and since Alex lives with their other parent on a permanent basis, the other parent would be the person considered responsible for Alex and would be the appropriate claimant. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 10), who lives permanently with their other parent. While the claimant contributes £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is below the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an only child. Since the claimant doesn't live with Alex and isn't contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit, they don't meet the responsibility test. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the other parent who Alex lives with would be the appropriate claimant.
